coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel β-cyclodextrin functionalized S, N codoped carbon dots for selective detection of testosterone
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Mai
Hua, Yifan
Liang, Yiran
Han, Jiajun
Liu, Donghui
Zhao, Wenting
Wang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: A novel functionalized carbon dot has been synthesized by covalently linking β-cyclodextrin to the surface of N, S codoped carbon dots (β-CD-CDs). The characterization was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, infrared spectra, ultraviolet-visible, and fluorescence emission spectra. On the basis of this carbon dot and (ferrocenylmethyl) trimethylammonium iodide (Fc+), a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) fluorescent probe system was developed to determine the concentration of testosterone in water and identify testosterone in cell by fluorescence imaging as a visible biomarker. Under the optimum condition, the fluorescent intensity of the probe system linearly responded to the concentration of testosterone from 0μM to 280μM and the limit of detection was 0.51μM. This probe system also performed well at determining testosterone in groundwater with average recoveries of testosterone ranging from 96% to 107% at spiking levels of 0.5–100μM, and the relative standard deviation remained below 13%, which provided a reliable, rapid and easy method to determine testosterone in environmental water. Furthermore, the low cytotoxicity, high anti-interference ability, and excellent biocompatibility of β-CD-CDs made this probe system successfully used in cell fluorescence imaging to monitor levels of testosterone in the cytoplasm of cells with a promising application value in medical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-cyclodextrin
N, S codoped carbon dot
Testosterone
Selective determination
Cell fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S codoped carbon dots
Synthesis of β-cyclodextrin modified carbon dots
Construction of β-CD-CDs-Fc+ host–guest fluorescent probe system
Selectivity and interference test of β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe system
Determination of testosterone in environmental water
PET process between Fc+ and β-CD-CDs
Effects of pH on the fluorescent properties of the β-CD-CDs
Response of the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe system to steroid hormones
Specificity of the probe system to testosterone
Determination of testosterone with probe system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Young Teacher Science Fund of Beijing University of Agriculture
Young Teacher Science Fund of Da Bei Nong Group
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Contract Grants: 21677175, 21337005), supported by Chinese Universities Scientific Fund 2017LX001, supported by the Young Teacher Science Fund of Da Bei Nong Group (15ZK002) and the Young Teacher Science Fund of Beijing University of Agriculture (2017516006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the β-CD-CDs. Inset: typical β-CD-CDs with lattice parameter of 0.23nm. (b) FTIR spectra of β-CD, CDs, and β-CD-CDs. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectra of β-CD-CDs and Fc+, and the emission spectrum of β-CD-CDs obtained under maximum excitation at 355nm.

The fluorescent intensities (λex = 355nm, λem = 425nm) of β-CD-CDs versus the concentrations of Fc+. The concentrations of Fc+ were 0, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, and 400μM, respectively. Both β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1) and Fc+ dissolved in a phosphate buffer solution (10mM, pH 8.0).

The fluorescent intensities (λex = 355nm, λem = 425nm) of β-CD-CDs fluorescent probe system interacting with eight steroid hormones, respectively, in phosphate buffer solution (3mL, pH 7.4). The concentrations of steroid hormones were 200 and 80μM, respectively. The fluorescent probe system was constructed by β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1) and Fc+ (320μM). ∆I = I-I0, where I0 and I are the fluorescent intensity of probe system in the absence and presence of steroid hormones. Date are presented as mean ± SEM, n = 5.

Fluorescence spectra of the β-CD-CDs fluorescent probe system in the presence of testosterone at various concentrations (1–12). The concentrations of testosterone were 40, 80, 120, 160, 200, 240, 280, 320, 360, 400, 440, and 480μM. The fluorescent probe system was constructed by β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1) and Fc+ (320μM) in phosphate buffer solution (10mM, pH 7.4). Insert: the relationship between the enhanced fluorescent intensity (∆I) of the probe system at 425nm and the concentration of testosterone. ∆I = I-I0, where I0 and I are the fluorescent intensity of probe system in the absence and presence of testosterone. Date are presented as mean ± SEM, n = 5.

Fluorescence microscopy images of 293T cells were incubated with Fc+ pretreated β-CD-CDs (0.1mgmL−1) for 24h and then treated with PBS (a, b and c) or testosterone (d, e and f). Bright-field (left), dark-field fluorescence (middle), and overlap images of dark and bright field (right), respectively.
Synthesis β-CD functionalized N, S codoped CDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel β-cyclodextrin functionalized S, N codoped carbon dots for selective detection of testosterone

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Centre for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Department of Applied Chemistry, China Agricultural University, No.2 West Yuanmingyuan Road, Beijing 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Centre for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Department of Applied Chemistry, China Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nutritional Sciences and Toxicology, University of California, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nutritional Sciences and Toxicology, University of California

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biological Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Agriculture, 7# Beinong Road, Changping District, Beijing 102206, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biological Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel functionalized carbon dot has been synthesized by covalently linking β-cyclodextrin to the surface of N, S codoped carbon dots (β-CD-CDs). The characterization was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, infrared spectra, ultraviolet-visible, and fluorescence emission spectra. On the basis of this carbon dot and (ferrocenylmethyl) trimethylammonium iodide (Fc+), a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) fluorescent probe system was developed to determine the concentration of testosterone in water and identify testosterone in cell by fluorescence imaging as a visible biomarker. Under the optimum condition, the fluorescent intensity of the probe system linearly responded to the concentration of testosterone from 0μM to 280μM and the limit of detection was 0.51μM. This probe system also performed well at determining testosterone in groundwater with average recoveries of testosterone ranging from 96% to 107% at spiking levels of 0.5–100μM, and the relative standard deviation remained below 13%, which provided a reliable, rapid and easy method to determine testosterone in environmental water. Furthermore, the low cytotoxicity, high anti-interference ability, and excellent biocompatibility of β-CD-CDs made this probe system successfully used in cell fluorescence imaging to monitor levels of testosterone in the cytoplasm of cells with a promising application value in medical research.

ce:section.ce:para: Testosterone is the principal steroid hormone from the androgenic group. As an important sex hormone, it not only participates in male sexual differentiation, development, and function but also involved in other aspects of life, such as physical appearance, behavior, mentality (Kim et al., 2016), and even social status. Recent research has proved that the level of testosterone is associated with many diseases (Zitzmann and Nieschlag, 2001), including, for example, type 2 diabetes (Grossmann et al., 2015), prostate cancer (Black et al., 2014), coronary artery disease (Nettleship et al., 2008; Wu and von Eckardstein, 2003), and kidney stone disease (Changtong et al., 2016). Hence, accurate measurements of the level and distribution of testosterone in the tissue and the cell is essential for understanding reproductive physiology as well as revealing how it influences the health of human beings. The conventional techniques for the analysis of testosterone include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Elder and Lewis, 1985), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Zhai and Lu, 2013), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Janssens et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2015), high-performance liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) (Lee et al., 2013), and ultra-performance liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) (Wang et al., 2016; You et al., 2011). Although these methods could fully meet the demands of sensitivity and selectivity in the assays of testosterone, they require tedious sample pretreatments and sophisticated equipment. Therefore, it remains a challenge to develop a sensitive, selective, rapid, simple, inexpensive, and reliable detection method for testosterone.

In the past several years, continuing interest has been focused on the development of fluorescent probes for the rapid and simple detection of a wide variety of analytes. Among all of these emerging fluorescent probes, semiconductor nanocrystals, also called quantum dots (QDs), are a kind of nano material that has a broad absorption spectrum, size-dependent emission, high quantum yield, and high photostability. These superior optical properties make it an ideal fluorescent probe, and it can be used successfully to detect several ions and organic compounds (Kuang et al., 2011; Lu et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2014). The main drawbacks of QDs, however, lie in their complicated and expensive synthesis routes, high toxicity (Derfus et al., 2004; Hardman, 2006), and low biocompatibility (Gerion et al., 2001). As a new alternative to QDs, carbon dots (CDs), because of their low cytotoxicity, good water solubility, chemical inertness, easy preparation, and environmental friendliness, are attracting considerable attention (Baker and Baker, 2010). Most of the obtained CDs, however, have a relatively low fluorescent quantum yield (FLQY, usually less than 50%) in comparison with traditional QDs. To improve this point, some researchers have doped different atoms into CDs. Because these atoms have different atomic orbits compared with carbon, which can form a new surface state to trap excited electrons and can increase density to dramatically increase the FLQY (Dong et al., 2013).

Recently, the use of fluorescent-doped CDs as chemosensors used to detect biomolecules and metal ions also has been reported based on the quenching mechanism of CD fluorescence (Costas-Mora et al., 2014; da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Dong et al., 2012a; Zhu et al., 2014). Because the surface of CDs lacks a specific interaction with target analytes, the detection often is disrupted by other molecular that can quench the fluorescence of CDs through an effective electron or energy transfer process. To overcome this problem, CDs need to be functionalized through their surface carboxylic acid and amino moieties to increase selectivity (Dong et al., 2012b; Gonçalves et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015; Yuan et al., 2014).

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD), because of its special molecular structure consisting of a hydrophobic internal cavity and a hydrophilic external surface, has been used extensively as a host molecule. The competitive host–guest interaction between β-CD with the dye molecule and target analytes has been used to design sensors (Ai et al., 2012; Ding et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2010; Tang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015). On the basis of the fluorescence quenching properties of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between quantum or carbon dots and dye molecule, a lot of β-CD functionalized probe systems have been constructed. Moreover, some researchers found that the fluorescence of β-CD functionalized quantum or carbon dots can be effectively quenched via photo-induced electron transfer (PET) in the presence of some molecules which have specific molecular orbits to accept the excited electron from quantum or carbon dots (Palaniappan et al., 2004). The application of those molecules that provide a new way to develop fluorescence probe system.

In the present study, the host–guest system complex of β-cyclodextrin and (ferrocenylmethyl) trimethylammonium iodide (Fc+) was used to functionalize N, S codoped CDs (β-CD-CDs-Fc+). The photo-induced electron transfer process between CDs and Fc+ can be interrupted by testosterone based on the competitive host–guest interaction, which means the system's fluorescent intensity will increase to nearly its original level after testosterone has been added. When the PET process occurs between CDs and Fc+, the fluorescent intensity of CDs will decrease, in part because of an excited electron transfer to Fc+ rather than a return to ground state. Therefore, we developed a kind of fluorescence “turn-on” probe to rapidly and easily detect testosterone in environmental water with high sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ probe was used to reveal the distribution of testosterone in the cells under fluorescence microscopy, and this approach could offer a potential visible biomarker to detect testosterone in cell fluorescence imaging.

All chemicals obtained from commercial suppliers were used without further purification. Ethanol, albumin bovine serum (BSA, 98.0%), 17α-hydroxy progesterone (98.0%), progesterone (98.0%), testosterone (98.0%), androstenedione (99.0%), β-estradiol (98.0%), medroxyprogesterone acetate (98.0%), megestrol acetate (96.0%), and estrone (98.0%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). L-Cysteine (97.0%) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98.0%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC⋅HCl, 98.0%) and (ferrocenylmethyl) trimethylammonium iodide (Fc+, 96.0%) were obtained from TCI Chemicals (Shanghai, China). Mono-(6-Amino-6-Deoxy)-β-Cyclodextrin (98.0%) was obtained from Zhiyuan Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shandong, China), and other chemicals and regents of analytical grade were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was prepared using a Milli-Q water system (18.2MΩcm−1) from a Millipore purification system (Barnstead Corp., MA).
Standard stock solutions of the steroid were prepared by dissolving the required amounts in ethanol to obtain a 10mM concentration of each compound, and subsequently stored refrigerated at 4⁰C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S codoped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: N, S codoped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized by thermal treatment of citric acid and L-cysteine according to the reported method with minor modification (Dong et al., 2013). Briefly, citric acid monohydrate (2g, 9.5mmol) and L-cysteine (1g, 8.3mmol) were dissolved in water (5mL) followed by evaporation at 70°C until dry. The resulting thick syrup was heated hydrothermally in a polytetrafluoroethylene-equipped stainless-steel autoclave at 200°C for 3h. The black syrup product then was diluted to 100mL after neutralized with 1molL−1 sodium hydroxide solution. After centrifugation at 4000rpm for 5min, the dark yellow solution was filtrated through a 0.22µm filter membrane and then was dialyzed with membrane (3500Da) for 48h to remove the salt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of β-cyclodextrin modified carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize β-CD-CDs, we added EDC⋅HCl (43mg) and NHS (51mg) into the CD solution (30mL, 0.5mgmL−1). The mixture was stirred for 30min to activate the carboxy groups on the surface of the CDs at room temperature. Mono-(6-amino-6-deoxy)-β-cyclodextrin (200mg) was then added to the mixture and reacted for 12h. Finally, the resulting solution was purified with dialysis membrane (3500Da) for 24h to remove unlinked β-CD, excessive EDC⋅HCl, NHS, and any by-products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of β-CD-CDs-Fc+ host–guest fluorescent probe system
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the amount of Fc+ to achieve the fluorescence quenching effect, the solution (3.0mL) containing β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1) and various amounts of Fc+ (0–400μM) in a phosphate buffer solution (10mM, pH 8.0) was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy at an optimal excitation wavelength (λEx) and recorded the fluorescent intensity changes at 425nm. Every time a certain amount of Fc+ was added, it was necessary to shake and incubate the solution for 20min. All of the experiments were conducted at room temperature. The obtained optimized β-CD-CDs-Fc+ host–guest fluorescent probe was used for the experiment.

We first assessed the testosterone by extending the incubation time with a certain amount of testosterone. The detection system solution (3.0mL, pH 7.4) containing β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1), Fc+ (320μM) and testosterone (350μM) was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescent signals (λEx 355nm) were collected from 0 to 60min after testosterone has been added to the probe system. Under the optimal incubation time (20min), we determined the effect of the concentration of the testosterone on the performance of the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ host–guest fluorescent probe system as follows: The mixtures of testosterone and the probe in buffer solutions (pH 7.4) with final concentrations of 0–480μM testosterone were prepared, and then their fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 355nm. The experiment was conducted at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference test of β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe system
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe, seven different steroid hormones, which have a similar molecular structure with testosterone, were mixed with previously optimized probe in the buffer solution (pH 8.0) with final concentrations of 80μM and 200μM, respectively. The fluorescence of the probe solution containing β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1) and Fc+ (320μM) was regarded as the control. To verify the potential of the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe to serve as a kind of visible biomarker of testosterone in cells, we mixed some common coexisting substances (K+, NO3 -, ascorbic acid, glucose, L-tryptophan, D-tryptophan, serine, and BSA) in cells with different final concentrations (1000, 1000, 100, 100,100, 100, 100, and 5μM, respectively) and testosterone (200μM) with a probe in the buffer solution (pH 8.0). The fluorescence of the detection system containing β-CD-CDs (20μgmL−1), Fc+ (320μM), and testosterone (200μM) was used as the control. All of these experiments were conducted at room temperature, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 355nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of testosterone in environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: Real water samples (ground water, free of the target analyte) filtered through a 0.22-mm membrane to remove any particulate matter and maintained in the dark at 4 ⁰C until analysis. Testosterone was directly spiked into 100mL water sample over a range 0.5–100μM, and allowed to stand for 2h at room temperature to distribute evenly. Under the optimal condition, 3mL of spiked water sample was added into a 5mL tube, and then 100μL Fc+ and β-CD-CDs pre-mixture solution was added to get the final concentrations of them are 320μM and 20μgmL−1, respectively. The mixture was vortexed vigorously for 15s, and then incubated 20min at room temperature before their fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 355nm.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument (Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The specimens were prepared by drop-casting the sample solution onto copper TEM grids coated with a holey amorphous carbon film. We obtained X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data using an ESCALab250 electron spectrometer from Thermo Scientific Corporation (Waltham, MA) with monochromatic 150W Al-Kα radiation. The base pressure was about 6.5 × 10−10 mbar. The binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.8eV from adventitious carbon. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra data were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). The photoluminescence spectra-related experiments were conducted on a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a 100 FT-IR spectrophotometer from Perkin-Elmer (Waltham, MA).

The synthesis route and detection strategy is shown in Scheme 1. The carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the CDs were activated by EDC and NHS to react with the amino groups of mono-(6-amino-6-deoxy)-β-cyclodextrin, which made CDs covalently link to β-cyclodextrin through a –CO–NH– bond. This is confirmed in the FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 1b). In the IR spectra of β-CD-CDs, the strong band at 1155cm−1 and the bands at 1079cm−1 1031cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric glycosidic vibration υa (C–O–C) and the coupled stretching vibration υ (C-C and C-O), respectively, which also can be considered to be the characteristic band of β-CD. The C = O bond of carboxylic acid groups at 1702cm−1 in CDs vanishes, and the asymmetric vibration of COO- at 1632cm−1 in CDs shifts to 1643cm−1. Both of these findings indicate the formation of an amide bond. This also can be confirmed by XPS spectra data (Fig. S1), which contains an N-C = O binding energy signal at 399.3eV in the β-CD-CDs. The binding energy signal at 284.5eV confirms the graphitic structure (sp2 C-C) of the β-CD-CDs. The signal at about 285.5eV suggests the presence of C-O, C-S, and C-N, and the signal around 288.0eV can be assigned to C=O.

We investigated the morphology of β-CD-CDs using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the size distribution of β-CD-CD probes ranged from 3nm to 7nm. The lattice spacing of β-CD-CDs is 0.23nm, which is in agreement with the in-plane lattice spacing pattern of graphene (100 facet), indicating that the grafted β-CD did not change the nanostructure of CDs (Dong et al., 2013; Tang et al., 2012).

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of β-CD-CDs is shown in Fig. 1c. The absorption and emission bands of β-CD-CDs are at 355nm and 425nm, which is similar to the CDs. The FLQY (see Table S1 in the Supplemental information), however, of β-CD-CDs is calculated to be 67.3% by selecting quinine sulfate as the standard, which is a little higher than the FLQY of CDs (63.2%). One reasonable explanation is that the modification of the β-CD may passivate the surface of the CDs, resulting in a hypsochromic emission and a higher quantum yield (Myung et al., 2003).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PET process between Fc+ and β-CD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: When β-CD functionalized the CDs, it introduced a hydrophobic environment on the surface of the CDs. Because of the unique molecular structure of Fc+ (i.e., the size is well matched with the cavity of β-CD), it could rapidly enter the cavity. This process could reduce the distance between the iron core of Fc+ and the surface of the CDs. Therefore, the photo-induced excited electron would effectively transfer to the empty molecular orbits of Fc+. As the results shown in Fig. 2, the fluorescent intensity of β-CD-CDs decreased with the amount of Fc+; however, the fluorescent intensity remained almost stable after Fc+ above 320μM. This might be due to the fact that the cavity of β-CD already has been occupied by the Fc+, and there was no more room for new Fc+ to reduce the distance with CDs to make PET occur, which was crucial for the excited PET process. To verify this hypothesis, we mixed original CDs with Fc+ to measure the change in the fluorescence spectra. The results in Fig. S2 show that Fc+ has only a slight quench effect on the CDs compared with the β-CD-CDs. This means that the grafted β-CD on the surface of the CDs not only provides an anchor point for Fc+ on the surface of the CDs but also forms a bridge to facilitate the PET process. The β-CD also acts as a kind of host molecular, however, and the Fc+ can be removed from the cavity by the competitive host–guest interaction between β-CD and other molecules. When the Fc+ was removed by other molecules, the PET process also was cut off, which “turned on” the fluorescence of β-CD-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on the fluorescent properties of the β-CD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a potential biomarker of testosterone, β-CD-CD should demonstrate stable performance when used in different samples that have a various range of pH. Fig. S3a shows the effects of pH (5.16, 6.00, 7.03, 7.97, and 9.05) on the fluorescence emission intensity of β-CD-CDs. Intensity increases by only a little. Even after Fc+ has been added to form the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe system, the fluorescent intensity still remains stable (Fig. S3b). Therefore, under the common range of pH in the cell, the pH of the phosphate buffer solution has nearly no effect on the fluorescent performance of the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe system. Because most cells live in the neutral or slightly basic conditions, the pH condition of following experiments was selected as 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the β-CD-CDs-Fc+ fluorescent probe system to steroid hormones
Specificity of the probe system to testosterone
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent response of the probe system with seven steroid hormones, including 17α-hydroxy progesterone (OHP), progesterone (P4), testosterone (T), androstenedione (A4), β-estradiol (E2), medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA), megestrol acetate (MGA), and estrone (E1), were conducted to examine selectivity. The fluorescent intensity changes of the probe system with these steroid hormones have been investigated at two concentration levels (80μM and 200μM), and the results are shown in Fig. 3. Obviously, the fluorescent intensities of the probe system responded significantly to testosterone at both concentration levels; however, for the rest of the steroid hormones, they remained nearly unchanged. Only progesterone and androstenedione slightly increased the probe system's fluorescent intensities at 200μM. This highly selective response could result from the difference in the competitive binding constant (Bednarek et al., 2002; Sadlej-Sosnowska, 1995, 1996). Above results suggest that the probe system has a promising potential to selectively determine the testosterone in a wide range of concentration levels, and this selectivity is not disturbed easily by other steroid hormones.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of testosterone with probe system
ce:section.ce:para: When testosterone was added to the probe system, it would displace Fc+ in the cavity of β-CD by a competitive host–guest interaction and then formed a new β-CD-testosterone inclusion complex. As in Fig. S4, the fluorescent intensity increased with increasing incubation time and remained stable for 20min. This means the competitive interaction would achieve a state of equilibrium after 20min.

Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence recovery spectra of the probe system with various concentrations of testosterone in the range of 0μM to 500μM. When the concentration of testosterone was greater than 300μM, the fluorescent intensity remained nearly constant. The insert in Fig. 4 shows the relationship between the concentration of testosterone and the enhanced fluorescent intensity (ΔI = I-I0) of the system, where I0 and I represent the fluorescent intensities of the probe system in the absence and presence of testosterone. In the concentration of testosterone ranging from 0μM to 280μM, the enhanced fluorescent intensity of the probe system versus the concentration presents satisfactory linearity. The linear regression equation is as follows: ΔI = 0.8311C (Tes) + 0.6942 with a regression correlation of 0.9962. The limit of detection (LOD), based on 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal, and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot, for the probe system is 0.51μM. These findings indicate that the probe system can be utilized as a reliable method to selectively and sensitively determine testosterone levels.

Considering the endocrine disruption of those steroid hormones, the content of the steroid hormones in environmental water usually was very low. To evaluate the accuracy and repeatability of the probe system in the environmental analysis field, we monitored the level of testosterone in the groundwater samples. We performed different spiking levels of testosterone (0.5, 10, 100μM) in the testosterone-free river water samples. Before the spiking, these water samples were filtered through 0.2mm membrane to remove any particulate matter. As seen from the summarized data in Table S2, the average recoveries of testosterone ranged from 96% to 107% and the relative standard deviations (RSD) was lower than 13%, which satisfied the requirements of residue analysis. We compared the specific analytical features of this probe system with others existing methods. As shown is Table S5, although the LOD of the probe system for testosterone is higher than previously reported for HPLC-MS (Lee et al., 2013) or GC-MS (Janssens et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2015), considering the lack of any time consuming sample pretreatment procedures to enrich the target compound, this probe system–based spectrophotometric method is suitable for the routine rapid determination of testosterone in environmental water samples.

To verify the potential of this probe system to be used as a biomarker, the anti-interference ability of the established PET system for testosterone detection using various coexistence substances were further evaluated, which commonly were added in cell cultural media. As shown in Table S3, the interference effect of those common coexistence substances on the fluorescent intensity of this probe system was negligible (∆I/I < 3.1%). The results indicated that little interference came from the substances coexisting in these biological samples. Therefore, the β-CD-CDs fluorescent biosensor has potential application for cell imaging.

Before the β-CD-CDs could be applied to the cell images, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of this nano-materials by MTT assay. As shown in Table S4 the inhibition rates at different β-CD-CDs concentrations, the IC50 was 4.8mgmL−1, which was significantly higher than the concentration that was applied in the probe system. The Fc+ pretreated β-CD-CDs was incubated with 293T cells for 24h before the cells were treated with testosterone (see the Supplemental information). As shown in the fluorescent microscopy images in Fig. 5a–f, because of small size and good membrane permeability, the β-CD-CDs are well-distributed in the cytoplasm of cells. The overlay image of the fluorescent and bright microphotograph in Fig. 5c and f indicated that the Fc+ pretreated β-CD-CDs continued to have a weak fluorescence signal in the cells; however, after the cells were treated with testosterone, the Fc+ was removed from β-CD-CDs, increasing the fluorescence signal. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity change area in the cell could indicate the location at which drug release occurs. Therefore, if PET occurs between β-CD-CDs and drugs, when drug has been released from β-CD-CDs, the fluorescent intensity of β-CD-CDs will change. On the basis of this phenomenon, we then can monitor whether this release occurs.

In this paper, a novel high-fluorescent quantum yield probe system based on competitive host–guest interaction and photo-induced electron transfer has been constructed to determine testosterone. The importance of β-cyclodextrin for the probe system was demonstrated. As a receptor of Fc+ and testosterone, β-cyclodextrin not only specifically recognized testosterone, but also captured Fc+ to make PET occur. This highly selective response to testosterone in a wide range of concentration levels ensured that this probe system could be used to rapidly and easily determine testosterone in environmental water samples at a trace level when combined with enrichment pretreatment procedures. Furthermore, the low-cytotoxicity β-CD-CDs with Fc+ in the cavity were incubated with 293T cells as visible biomarker to monitor testosterone level. The successful application of this probe system in cell fluorescence imaging filed might provide a new avenue to utilize this kind of probe system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Contract Grants: 21677175, 21337005), supported by Chinese Universities Scientific Fund 2017LX001, supported by the Young Teacher Science Fund of Da Bei Nong Group (15ZK002) and the Young Teacher Science Fund of Beijing University of Agriculture (2017516006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.06.056.
